content:
First, observe that any integer choice \(A_i\) must lie in \(\{2,3\}\), because choosing \(1\) or \(4\) would already incur an error \(\ge1.14\), far worse than what we can do with just \(2\)s and \(3\)s.  Since
\[
\sum a_i=19
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum A_i=19,
\]
and each \(A_i\in\{2,3\}\), if we let \(x\) be the number of 3’s, then
\[
3x+2(7-x)=19
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x=5,\quad 7-x=2.
\]
So we must choose exactly five of the \(A_i\)’s to be \(3\) and two of them to be \(2\).

Compute the “down‐round” errors \(e^{(2)}_i=a_i-2\) and “up‐round” errors \(e^{(3)}_i=3-a_i\):

i :   1     2     3     4     5     6     7  
a_i: 2.56, 2.61, 2.65, 2.71, 2.79, 2.82, 2.86  
e^{(2)}:0.56,0.61,0.65,0.71,0.79,0.82,0.86  
e^{(3)}:0.44,0.39,0.35,0.29,0.21,0.18,0.14  

To keep the maximum error \(M\) as small as possible, one “down‐round” (to 2) wants the two smallest \(e^{(2)}\), namely
\[
e^{(2)}_1=0.56,\quad e^{(2)}_2=0.61,
\]
and the five “up‐round”s (to 3) want the five smallest \(e^{(3)}\), whose largest among those five is
\[
\max\{e^{(3)}_3,e^{(3)}_4,e^{(3)}_5,e^{(3)}_6,e^{(3)}_7\}
=\max\{0.35,0.29,0.21,0.18,0.14\}=0.35.
\]
Thus under the optimal choice (round \(a_1,a_2\) down to 2, all the rest up to 3) the worst‐case error is
\[
M=\max(0.61,0.35)=0.61.
\]
Hence
\[
100M=100\cdot0.61=\boxed{61}.
\]

\[
\sum a_i=19
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\sum A_i=19,
\]

\[
3x+2(7-x)=19
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x=5,\quad 7-x=2.
\]
So we must choose exactly five of the \(A_i\)’s to be \(3\) and two of them to be \(2\).

Compute the “down‐round” errors \(e^{(2)}_i=a_i-2\) and “up‐round” errors \(e^{(3)}_i=3-a_i\):

i :   1     2     3     4     5     6     7  
a_i: 2.56, 2.61, 2.65, 2.71, 2.79, 2.82, 2.86  
e^{(2)}:0.56,0.61,0.65,0.71,0.79,0.82,0.86  
e^{(3)}:0.44,0.39,0.35,0.29,0.21,0.18,0.14  

To keep the maximum error \(M\) as small as possible, one “down‐round” (to 2) wants the two smallest \(e^{(2)}\), namely
\[

\[
\max\{e^{(3)}_3,e^{(3)}_4,e^{(3)}_5,e^{(3)}_6,e^{(3)}_7\}

\[

\[
